summary: O'Neil reacts to news that five major banks pleaded guilty to manipulating foreign exchange markets, expressing frustration that she can't even keep track of all the financial fraud cases anymore. She points out that despite criminal guilty pleas, no one goes to jail and banks receive immediate waivers to continue business as usual. The Justice Department even delayed announcing charges so waivers could be granted without disrupting business. She argues that fines are meaningless when quantitative easing essentially gifts cash back to these same banks, calling the entire enforcement regime 'theater' designed to create the appearance of accountability.
key_points: Five major banks pleaded guilty to forex manipulation via a price-fixing cartel coordinated through chat rooms
Despite criminal guilty pleas, no individuals went to jail and banks received immediate SEC waivers to continue normal operations
The Justice Department delayed announcing charges specifically to ensure business wouldn't be disrupted
Fines are essentially meaningless when quantitative easing transfers cash back to the same banks
The repetitive nature of these scandals has created public fatigue, making it impossible to track all the fraud cases
money_quotes: If you aint cheating, you aint trying
Here I am, someone who is actively taking an interest in financial reform, and I actually can't remember all the fines, settlements, and fake guilty pleas to criminal charges.
I say 'fake' because – yet again – nobody has gone to jail, and the banks found guilty have immediately been given waivers by the SEC to continue business as usual.
The Justice Department even delayed announcing the charges by a week so those waivers could be granted in time so that business wouldn't even be disrupted. For fuck's sake.
The continued quantitative easing has essentially been a gift of cash to those same banks, so I wouldn't even count the fines as meaningful.
In fact I'd call this whole thing theater. And really repetitive, boring theater at that, where we all nod off because every scene is the same and they've turned up the heat too high.
I'd argue that we've actually gone backwards on incentives not to commit fraud, since now everything has been formalized as pathetic.
We will all be so bored we won't even keep track, because nobody can.
key_insight: The sheer volume and repetitiveness of big bank fraud cases, combined with the theater of meaningless fines and immediate waivers, has been designed to exhaust public attention rather than actually punish or deter criminal behavior.
